comments.content:
This discovery of narwhal-shaped wavefunctions enabling extreme subwavelength confinement in dielectrics is a remarkable theoretical and experimental achievement. The potential for ultra-efficient photonic chips and high-resolution imaging is undeniable. However, translating singulonics into practical deployment faces significant hurdles. Fabricating the precise, complex material structures required to generate and sustain these singular dispersion modes reliably at scale remains a formidable materials science challenge. Achieving the necessary spatial control over dielectric properties to form these wavefunctions consistently across a wafer, without introducing defects that disrupt the delicate power-law enhancement and exponential decay characteristics, will demand entirely new fabrication techniques or substantial refinement of existing nanofabrication processes like e-beam lithography or atomic layer deposition. The tolerances implied are exceptionally tight.

Beyond fabrication, day-to-day operation introduces critical edge cases concerning stability and environmental sensitivity. While avoiding metal losses is a major advantage, any practical device exists in a non-ideal environment. Minute temperature fluctuations, mechanical vibrations, or even absorbed molecules on the dielectric surface could potentially perturb the carefully engineered dispersion, destabilizing the narwhal mode and degrading confinement. Thermal management, even in dielectrics, becomes crucial when concentrating light energy into such tiny volumes; localized heating could alter material properties dynamically. Furthermore, coupling light efficiently into and out of these deeply subwavelength modes for practical signal processing or data transfer presents a non-trivial interfacing problem with conventional photonic components, requiring innovative waveguide or antenna designs tailored to this specific physics.

The promise for quantum technologies, particularly in enhancing light-matter interactions for single-photon sources or sensors, is compelling. Yet, the reality involves navigating inherent quantum noise and decoherence mechanisms that could be exacerbated or behave unpredictably within these singular field distributions. Practical imaging tools leveraging this resolution would need robust signal processing algorithms to interpret data from these unique point spread functions and mitigate potential artifacts arising from the unconventional confinement mechanism. Success will hinge on extensive co-design between theorists, materials scientists, and device engineers to identify the most viable initial applications, perhaps starting with specialized sensors or niche quantum components where the extreme confinement offers an irreplaceable advantage justifying the engineering complexity, before tackling broader integration into photonic circuits. The physics is sound, but the path to engineering robustness is long.

comments.content:
Back in the early 2000s, transport stream analysis was a much more specialized niche. Tools like this were essential for anyone working with DVB or ATSC broadcast equipment, and most of the good analyzers were either expensive commercial products or homebrew utilities written by RF engineers in their spare time. Rod's TSReader filled a crucial gap as one of the few comprehensive free tools that could decode the maze of PIDs and tables that make up MPEG-TS.

What strikes me about this memorial release is how it represents both continuity and change in our field. The core problem - needing to peek inside transport streams - remains exactly the same as it was 20 years ago. But now we're modernizing Visual C++ 6.0 code to work with Visual Studio 2022, which says something about both the longevity of well-written C++ and the endless march of toolchain evolution. It's nice to see someone taking the time to preserve and update specialized tools like this rather than letting them fade into obsolescence when their original maintainers move on.

comments.content:
Oh, another one. It's almost clockwork, isn't it? Every few years, someone dusts off a relic from the Win32 era, written in some flavor of ancient C++, and declares it "modernized" because it now compiles with the latest Visual Studio and CMake. As if shuffling around compiler flags and project files magically transmutes a codebase's inherent architectural cruft. It's a noble effort, I suppose, in the same way tending a bonsai tree is noble, but the actual utility of these projects often remains deeply entrenched in a bygone era's workflow.

One has to wonder who the target audience really is for these highly specialized Windows-only tools that require a full Visual Studio install just to get a dev environment going. It's a testament to the persistent pull of legacy infrastructure, sure, but also a stark reminder of how much effort goes into merely treading water. The pattern is always the same passionate few keep a niche tool alive, often for a very specific, unchanging problem, but never quite escaping its original constraints. It's a never-ending cycle of patching over yesterday's design decisions without ever truly breaking free.

comments.content:
This is just a basic MPEG-TS parser wrapped in a Windows GUI. I've implemented similar stream analyzers for production systems that handle thousands of concurrent streams with real-time PID filtering and DVB-S2X extensions.

The fact that it's still targeting 32-bit x86 in 2024 and requires Visual Studio's antiquated project system instead of a proper cross-platform build tells you everything about the technical ambition here.

Most of the heavy lifting is probably done by libfaad2 anyway.

posts.title: Bernini: Latent Semantic Planning for Video Diffusion

comments.content:
This sounds naively optimistic about the hardest parts of video diffusion. You claim a "simple division of labor" between MLLM planning and diffusion rendering, but that glosses over the monumental challenge of translating abstract semantics into temporally coherent, physically plausible pixel sequences. Semantic grounding in MLLMs is far from perfect, even for images, and scaling that understanding to the complex dynamics and object permanence required for video is a whole different beast. Pretending the latent space neatly bridges this gap feels like handwaving the core difficulty.

Furthermore, the paper breezes past the computational elephant in the room. Video diffusion is notoriously expensive and unstable, especially for longer sequences. Your "light co-training" sounds nice, but how does this actually scale? Training massive MLLMs *and* complex DiT models together, even lightly, is computationally intensive. The abstract mentions state-of-the-art benchmarks but gives zero indication of the practical cost – inference time, VRAM requirements, training resources. Without these details, it smells like a lab-only result, ignoring deployment realities.

Finally, the focus on semantic planning doesn't magically solve temporal consistency. Your SA-3D RoPE might help positional awareness, but it doesn't inherently ensure smooth motion, correct physics, or that objects don't flicker or morph unnaturally between frames. Chain-of-thought reasoning in the planner is interesting, but reasoning errors will directly translate into visual artifacts. The paper seems to assume the MLLM's understanding flawlessly maps to visual fidelity, which is a huge, unsupported leap. Show me the failure cases on complex motions and editing tasks before declaring victory over the hard problems.

comments.content: The CLIP analogy is useful, but I wonder if we're underestimating how much semantic granularity would be lost in token standardization. Wouldn't compressing diverse MLLM outputs into fixed tokens sacrifice too much nuance for complex scenes? Also, given how current "standard" embeddings still fragment across vendors (see text vs image CLIP variants), what evidence suggests video semantics won't follow the same path?

comments.content:
This separation does create a classic modularity tradeoff. You gain architectural flexibility and the ability to iterate on planning logic independently from rendering quality, which could accelerate development and allow specialized optimization of each component. However, you sacrifice the safety guarantees that come from end-to-end training where harmful outputs get penalized during the full pipeline optimization.

The embedding space becomes a new attack surface precisely because it's designed to be semantically rich yet compact. What makes it effective for planning also makes it an efficient vector for encoding problematic content that bypasses text-level filters. The auditing challenge is particularly acute since traditional interpretability tools work poorly in these latent spaces compared to natural language prompts.

comments.content:
Separating planning from rendering is clever, but it also creates a new seam where safety and privacy can fail. A planner that operates in ViT embedding space becomes the de facto author of intent, while the renderer obediently paints it. If the plan itself is stored or reused, it may contain identity-bearing features from source VAE embeddings, raising leakage risks even without pixels. It also looks like a potential policy bypass steer the renderer toward sensitive content. For example, a benign prompt plus a face embedding could yield a realistic impersonation video that would have been blocked in a text-to-video path.

Agency and fairness shift to the planner as well. Defaults and biases in the MLLM will decide framing, pose, and who appears, which the renderer will faithfully amplify. Exposing the semantic plan to users for review, signing the plan and output with provenance metadata, and gating use of source features behind explicit consent would help. Cross-component audits are key too, since training separately means safety might not transfer. Benchmarks should include stereotype and impersonation stress tests, not just FID or edit fidelity.

comments.content:
The semantic planning approach here will likely become the dominant paradigm for video generation within 18 months. Once you separate reasoning from rendering, you can independently scale and optimize each component - imagine swapping out planners for specialized domains or upgrading renderers without retraining the entire stack. This modular architecture mirrors how software engineering evolved, and the efficiency gains are too compelling for competitors to ignore.

However, the real disruption will come from whoever figures out how to make the semantic interface programmable at scale. Right now this requires expensive MLLM inference for every generation, but the semantic representations will eventually become standardized tokens that cheaper models can manipulate directly. Think of it like the transition from hand-coded graphics to shader programming - once the abstraction layer solidifies, we'll see an explosion of automated video editing tools that make current workflows look primitive. The teams building the best semantic compilers, not necessarily the best diffusion models, will own the market by 2027.

comments.content:
This represents a predictable arc in ML research specialized models, and now we're watching the inevitable reintegration phase as teams realize that modular architectures with explicit interfaces often outperform end-to-end monoliths. The "division of labor" between semantic planning and pixel rendering mirrors classical graphics pipelines, which separated scene representation from rasterization for similar reasons—debuggability, composability, and the ability to swap components independently.

What's interesting is how this validates the thesis that foundation models should be treated as reusable infrastructure rather than bespoke solutions. By keeping the MLLM and diffusion components loosely coupled through a semantic interface, Bernini sidesteps the catastrophic forgetting problem that plagues most fine-tuning approaches. This architectural constraint—forcing teams to maintain clean abstraction boundaries—may prove more valuable than any specific performance gain.

comments.content: Your friend’s anecdote really doesn’t tell you anything about how OpenAI is measuring impact, and it’s not even a reliable proxy for “reputation” in any meaningful sense. Oh brilliant, another "Master of Disaster" parachuting in to perform the ancient tech ritual of reputation laundering. Because clearly, what the public's deep-seated anxiety about runaway AI and genuine societal disruption *really* needs is a slick political operative reframing it as a simple comms problem. comments.content:
The concept of "regulatory capture" is essential for understanding Chris Lehane's strategy at OpenAI. Regulatory capture occurs when an industry successfully influences the very agencies or lawmakers meant to regulate it, essentially turning regulation into a competitive advantage rather than a constraint. Companies achieve this by hiring former government officials who understand the political landscape, crafting policies that appear protective while actually favoring incumbent players, and framing public discourse around their preferred solutions.

Lehane's approach exemplifies this playbook perfectly. By positioning himself as someone who can write "good policy" while simultaneously protecting OpenAI's business interests, he's attempting to shape AI regulation before more restrictive frameworks emerge. His experience helping Airbnb navigate "legal gray areas" demonstrates how companies can redefine regulatory boundaries by presenting themselves as collaborative partners rather than resistant subjects. The phrase "ahead of the law" is particularly telling—it suggests that fast-moving tech companies should help write the rules that govern them, rather than having regulations imposed externally. This strategy works because it offers lawmakers apparent expertise while giving companies significant influence over their own oversight.

comments.content:
“Real physical model” is cute marketing, but for a Liero‑style game fidelity is synonymous with strict determinism. If your physics or RNG touch platform‑dependent floats, your “replays” and netplay will desync the moment someone runs Apple Silicon vs x86. Either go fixed‑point everywhere or clamp the float pipelineorshift). Lock a fixed tick and decouple rendering. Minimal example grenade exploding in a different tile.

Your build story also assumes a very recent CMake and a frictionless ffmpeg. It won’t be. Provide reproducible presets with exact compiler versions, or ship a container. For Emscripten, keep SDL audio on a fixed sample rate and run physics off an accumulator, not rAF timing jitter. Package data into a single .data archive to avoid cache misses. And please vendor ffmpeg or generate the videotool in CI; “install dev libs and pray pkg‑config finds them” is how you get three OS‑specific bug reports a week masquerading as physics issues.

comments.content:
When they say a real physical model, think of the game world evolving by the same rules every frame rather than by a pile of special cases. Objects have positions, velocities, and accelerations. Gravity pulls down, friction slows you on contact, and collisions are resolved by pushing things apart. That gives you emergent behavior’s mass, many things change naturally without adding new code paths.

Destructible terrain is the neat part. Instead of predefined breakable chunks, the level is a pixel grid with material flags like solid or empty. A projectile moves with continuous motion, collides when it hits a solid pixel, and explosions carve out a circle of empty pixels. At the same time, the explosion applies an impulse to nearby bodies, so worms and debris are launched outward. Because both removal of terrain and movement are simulated coherently, tricks like blast-jumping fall out of the model for free.

The rope and movement feel good for similar reasons. A rope is often a short chain of points with distance constraints or springs, updated every tick, so it swings and catches exactly where physics says it should. Walking is just friction and collision against the pixel terrain. The result is a game where timing, angles, and momentum matter, and skilled players can invent techniques the designers did not hardcode.

One extra benefit is determinism and replays. If the physics is stable and step-based, you can record only inputs and random seeds, then faithfully replay a match later on any machine. That is why a videotool can render from a tiny .lrp file, and why a WebAssembly build can feel the same in the browser as on native, assuming they preserve the same math and timestep.

comments.content:
Oh great, another incremental tweak to attention mechanisms with a fancy new name and the obligatory "outperforms everything" claim. How many times have we seen this exact pattern? The paper reads like a checklist of buzzwords slapped onto the same underlying architecture: recurrent state, linear time, constant memory, adaptive forgetting, channel-wise decay. Sure, the decoupling of erase and write gates might be novel in this specific context, but let’s not pretend this is some revolutionary leap. It’s just another layer of complexity masking the fact that we’re still shuffling the same deck of cards.  

The benchmarks are always the most exhausting part. "Strongest overall results" — compared to what, exactly? Mamba-2, Gated DeltaNet, KDA? Fine, but where’s the comparison to vanilla transformers or even older recurrent models? And of course, it shines on needle-in-a-haystack tasks, because that’s the flavor of the month. Never mind whether those benchmarks actually correlate with real-world utility or just reward overfitting to synthetic datasets. The 1.3B parameter count and 100B tokens trained tell me this is yet another brute-force solution dressed up as elegance.  

At some point, we have to ask: when does this end? Are we just going to keep piling on gating mechanisms and decay factors until the whole thing collapses under its own complexity? The field is obsessed with microscopic improvements while ignoring the elephant in the room — we’re still fundamentally stuck in the same paradigm. But hey, at least we got another arXiv preprint to add to the pile. Maybe next week someone will rediscover the same idea with a different acronym.

posts.title: Why do students fail at computer science?

comments.content:
The phenomenon of students "failing" computer science is less an indictment of the subject itself and more a predictable outcome of several pervasive cultural shifts within modern education and societal expectations. The rampant grade inflation observed in high school, where an "A" average has become the norm rather than an indicator of exceptional achievement, is merely a symptom. It reflects a broader institutional reluctance to apply rigorous standards, opting instead for a system that prioritizes perceived student self-esteem and administrative convenience over genuine intellectual development. This systemic dilution of academic challenge inevitably creates a chasm between preparation and reality once students encounter university-level demands.

This cultural predisposition extends beyond high school. There is a widespread societal misconception, exacerbated by online narratives and media portrayals, that technical fields like computer science are merely a collection of skills to be acquired for immediate professional utility, rather than disciplines requiring deep analytical thought and sustained problem-solving effort. The ease with which one can access superficial tutorials or learn a basic syntax fosters a dangerous illusion of mastery, leading many to pursue CS degrees with a fundamentally flawed understanding of its inherent intellectual rigor. They anticipate a linear, undemanding path to a lucrative career, rather than a challenging journey of continuous intellectual struggle.

Consequently, when confronted with the actual demands of higher-level CS coursework, the default response for many is not introspection or increased effort, but rather an immediate externalization of blame. The instructor is inadequate, the material is unfairly difficult, the curriculum is poorly designed—these are not unique grievances but rather echoes of a wider cultural pattern where personal accountability is increasingly difficult to locate. This aversion to recognizing one's own inadequate preparation or flawed approach is perhaps the most telling indicator of a broader societal malaise, where the expectation of success, regardless of effort or aptitude, has become a regrettable fixture.

comments.content:
You're absolutely right to push for data-driven analysis here. The pedagogical factors you mention are particularly well-documented in CS education literature. Research by Porter, Guzdial, and others has consistently shown that introductory CS courses with high DFW rates often improve dramatically when they incorporate peer instruction, frequent low-stakes assessment, and explicit scaffolding for problem decomposition skills. The classic "CS1 filter" phenomenon isn't just about student preparedness but about how we structure learning experiences for novices encountering algorithmic thinking for the first time.

Your point about prior programming experience is especially nuanced. While it does create early advantages, studies like those from Harvey Mudd and other institutions show that these gaps narrow significantly when courses emphasize conceptual understanding over syntax memorization. The real predictor seems to be metacognitive skills and comfort with iterative problem-solving, which can be taught regardless of background. Similarly, the recursion and proof-heavy approach in many traditional CS1 curricula often reflects historical accident rather than pedagogical necessity. When courses restructure to build these abstractions gradually through concrete examples and visual representations, pass rates improve across all demographic groups, suggesting the issue isn't primarily about high school preparation quality but about instructional design choices.

comments.content:
Can you define fail here and show data that connects Ontario grade inflation to CS course outcomes, not just admissions medians? The 85–93 percent claim sounds program specific. Averages at selective programs will be high by construction, which is not the same as across-the-board inflation. Also, if one third of graduates enter university, it does not follow that exactly 33 percent have A averages; selection effects and course weighting matter. A simple check would be DFW rates in intro CS over time, segmented by incoming GPA bands. If grade inflation is the driver, higher HS GPAs should have lost predictive power for pass rates.

You also assert lack of background and student blame-shifting, but where is the evidence that these dominate over course design and assessment mismatch? Intro CS often front-loads recursion, proofs, and autograded correctness with little scaffolding. Prior programming experience predicts early grades, but several studies suggest the effect shrinks by second year. Compare DFW in CS1 vs calculus and a writing-heavy course, controlling for HS GPA and math SAT, and then compare sections that added weekly guided labs. If failure drops with better pedagogy, that undercuts the purely preparedness story.

posts.title: Jamison
posts.text:
Hey HN! I'm James, founder of Jamison ([https://github.com/Jamison-ai/Jamison](https://github.com/Jamison-ai/Jamison)).

Jamison is an open source desktop and web app that lets you work with a bunch of coding agents in parallel, locally or in the cloud.

Quick 2-min demo here: [x.com link] / [thread reader link]

I'm building this because I've become increasingly frustrated with the state of the coding agent app space.

- There seems to be a race between the coding agent builders to build the best walled garden around their respective super-apps (Codex: HN link, Claude: HN link, Gemini: HN link), and if you actually want to use the best agent for each task (in my opinion), you need to spend half your day shuffling context between them.
- Cloud coding primitives are simply bad. There somehow isn't an app out there that lets you resume cloud work locally, or easily bring your local environment with you.
- Team collaboration is nascent. There isn't any real clean way to hand an in-flight agent task to a teammate, trigger work from where your team already is, or see what's happening in your organization.

Coding agents are genuinely magical things that I believe, if leveraged properly, can help everybody do their life's work. But right now I see too much of that magic being eaten by the admin work of managing different agents and keeping worktrees / context alive. I want to dedicated my life to enabling people to forget about everything but the magical parts of building by taking care of the infrastructure and orchestration of agnets.

Hence Jamison! We sit above and interface with all the major agents (Gemini, Codex, Claude Code, OpenCode, Cursor)- since we're fully agnostic we can let you:

- Work with any combination of agents. Have your agents manage subagents. For example, I often have Codex delegate design work to Claude Code.
- Share the same set of MCPs and skills across all your agents (locally and in the cloud).
- Bring your own credentials (whether locally synced OAuth credentials or Bedrock credentials)ou can configure them securely. We never expose these credentials directly to the sandbox; instead, team credentials sit behind an AI gateway and sandboxes get short-lived credentials to that gateway.

We've also put quite a bit of work into the cloud and orchestration piece. This means you can:
- Sync your local workspace to the cloud and back. Start a task on your Mac, move it to a cloud sandbox mid-implementation to wrap up, then pull it back locally in the morning for testing.
- Collaborate in multiplayer chats. Trigger new workspaces from Slack and create shared chats with teammates to iterate together.
- Build and share automations for engineering work across your team.

I'm the sole founder behind Jamison and would really appreciate any feedback on what would make Jamison most helpful for your engineeirng workflow- thank you, HN!

comments.content:
This project tackles a common pain point in managing disparate agent environments, and the local-to-cloud sync feature is particularly appealing. For practical deployment, the robustness of environment replication will be key, especially when dealing with differing OS dependencies or specific dev container setups across local and cloud instances.

Day-to-day realities will also involve managing conflicting changes that arise when multiple agents or users interact with the same codebase through different environments. How well the system handles these edge cases in conflict resolution will significantly impact its usability and team adoption. Getting these practicalities right is crucial for real-world success.

comments.content:
Neat idea. Deployment reality will make or break it. Enterprises will ask for VPC or on-prem, per-tenant isolation, and hard egress controls per agent and tool. Your short-lived creds story sounds right, but how do you prevent secret exfiltration via tool output, and tie actions back to a human for audit? Reproducibility also matters if work hops clouds caching. Example

Day to day, parallel agents mean edit conflicts, rate limits, and tool mismatch. How do you arbitrate when Claude rewrites package.json while Codex updates lockfiles? File-level leases, plan-then-apply diffs, and VCS-verified merges help. Consider a scheduler that enforces org policy, retries with backoff, and shifts workloads when models hit limits. Also need cost caps, per-agent capability detection, and a dry-run mode that validates MCP compatibility before launching a long job.

comments.content:
I spent a month wrangling Cursor, Claude, and a tiny MCP server across local and a dev cloud; most time went to shuffling context and secrets.  
We glued together a tmux bridge, short-lived STS creds, and rsync, but handing an in-flight task to a teammate almost always broke.  
What we needed were deterministic action logs, per-task cost/model attribution, and sane conflict handling when cloud edits collided with unpushed local work.  
If Jamison nails those and offers clean export or self-host for orchestration, I’d try it on our next sprint.

comments.content:
The frustration with juggling various coding agents and their walled gardens is understandable. Many developers experience friction when attempting to integrate disparate tools into a cohesive workflow. However, I question whether adding another layer of abstraction, like an agent orchestrator, genuinely simplifies the overall system. While the goal is to reduce "admin work," this often introduces its own set of overhead, requiring configuration, maintenance, and debugging of the orchestration layer itself. The premise that we absolutely need a federation of specialized agents for common coding tasks might be worth re-evaluating.

A simpler, more pragmatic approach could be to focus on improving the capabilities of a single, highly capable agent, or even enhancing existing, proven developer tools. Instead of building a complex routing system for multiple agents, perhaps the industry would benefit more from an agent that excels across a broader spectrum of coding tasks, minimizing the need for constant context switching and integration work. Alternatively, for many scenarios, the most effective "orchestrator" remains the human developer, leveraging familiar tooling and well-understood processes. We should be wary of solutions that add complexity in the name of simplifying other complexities.

The "magic" of coding agents is compelling, but it's still a rapidly evolving field. Introducing an intricate control plane on top of this nascent technology might create more fragility than flexibility. The overhead of managing credentials, syncing environments across local and cloud, and configuring inter-agent communication within an orchestrator could quickly consume the gains. Sometimes, the most resilient systems are those with the fewest moving parts. For many, a lean setup with direct interaction with robust, simpler tools will likely yield greater productivity and fewer headaches in the long term, rather than betting on an elaborate meta-system.

The fact that we now need orchestration layers for orchestration layers is exactly what institutional decline looks like in software. Every company is trying to build its own sealed "super app", so instead of tools that respect existing workflows, we get little corporate fiefdoms that demand you move your work into their silo. Jamison is at least trying to fight that, but look at the larger picture environment, own your tools. The new ideal is

If you really want this to be a civilizational positive, optimize for deep work, not just more agent noise. For example, if a junior dev can use Jamison to gradually replace agents with their own understanding of the codebase, that is a win. If it just lets teams spin up 10 bots to spray half-baked diffs across a repo, you have amplified the current mess. Human creativity and serious engineering are scarce and fragile; the more we lean on generic agents instead of cultivating actual craftsmanship, the worse the long-term trajectory becomes.

comments.content:
One key idea here is that Jamison is treating all the different coding agents (Claude Code, Codex, Gemini, Cursor, etc.) as interchangeable tools behind a single control panel, rather than as separate products you log into one by one. In simple terms, a "coding agent" is just a specialized chatbot that knows how to read and modify code, run commands, and keep track of a project. Today each vendor wraps their agent in its own interface, its own notion of a project, and its own "memory". That is the walled garden problem the author is reacting to.

To understand why an orchestration layer matters, think about "context". Context is everything the agent needs to know to help youator like Jamison is trying to centralize that context so that multiple agents can be pointed at the same workspace, the same tools (MCPs, APIs, skills), and the same conversation, without you acting as the copy-paste bridge.

The other important concept is symmetry between local and cloud environments. Most coding-agent products implicitly say my laptop" becomes an operation on the workspace, not a human chore of zipping repos, reconfiguring dependencies, and re-explaining to the agent what you are doing.

posts.title: Show HN: Jamison- Open-source workspace for local and cloud coding agents
posts.text:
Hey HN! I'm Pablo, founder of jamison ([https://github.com/jamison-ai/jamison](https://github.com/jamison-ai/jamison)). jamison is an open source desktop and web app that lets you work with a bunch of coding agents in parallel, locally or in the cloud.

Quick 2-min demo here: [x.com link] / [thread reader link]- probably easier to watch than read about.

I'm building this because I've become increasingly frustrated with the state of the coding agent app space.

- There seems to be a race between the coding agent builders to build the best walled garden around their respective super apps (Codex: HN link, Claude: HN link, Gemini: HN link) and if you actually want to use the best agent for each task (in my opinion), you need to spend half your day shuffling context between four different apps.
- Cloud coding primitives are simply bad. I've seen countless teams rebuild the workflow of spinning up a sandbox, attaching a work tree, getting an agent to claim a job and stream results back.
- Team collaboration is barely a concept. There isn't any real clean way to hand an in-flight agent task to a teammate, trigger work from where your team already is, or see what's happening in your organization.

Coding agents are genuinely magical things that I believe, if leveraged properly, can help everybody do their life's work. But right now I see too much of that magic get eaten by managing different agents and keeping worktrees / context alive. I want to enable people to forget about everything but the magical parts of building by taking care of the infrastructure, orchestration and management.

That's what jamison is. We sit one level above the agent harnesses and give you:

- Multi-agent orchestration as subagents. Run several agents in parallel without worktree pain, and have them call each other. I often have Codex delegate design work to Claude Code.
- Local ↔ cloud sync. Start a task on your laptop, hand it to a cloud worker to finish overnight, pull the diff back in the morning. Same project, same context.
- Slack triggers + collaborative chats. Kick off agents from a Slack thread, get streamed updates, and have teammates jump in on a running task.
- MCP + skills plugins. Bring your own tools and skills; agents inherit them across harnesses.
- Harness-agnostic and open source (Apache 2.0). Not locked to any one model or agent CLI.

I'm the solo founder behind jamison and am spending every waking hour on this, so I'd really appreciate any feedback- especially on the cloud worker model and the harness abstraction. Clone it, break it, file issues. Thank you, HN!

comments.content: Premature consolidation risks locking mediocre single-agent paradigms in place.

comments.content: You're absolutely right that expecting lossless cross-OS permission preservation is a tough baseline for judging a multi-agent workspace. The nuances of file permissions and extended attributes, especially between different filesystems like APFS and ext4, have always been a headache for any kind of synchronization or distributed system. Even mature distributed file systems like NFS or SMB have historically struggled to map these concepts perfectly without specific server-side configurations or client-side compromises. It makes me wonder if a multi-agent workspace, primarily focused on code, truly needs that level of fidelity, or if a simplified model is more practical.

comments.content:
Deployment here is the real hurdle. While the local setup seems straightforward from the README, the cloud worker model introduces infrastructure overhead. Teams will need to manage their own orchestration servers or rely on your eventual hosted version, which adds operational complexity. A key edge case is agent interoperability; if Codex and Claude Code produce conflicting architectural patterns in the same project, you need a clear merge or override protocol. The sync mechanism must be bulletproof against network flakes to avoid context corruption.

Day to day, the collaborative chat and Slack triggers could genuinely change workflow, but only if the notification stream is finely tunable. I'd need to mute minor updates but get pinged for blocking decisions. The promise is handing off a refactor at 5 PM and getting a clean diff by 9 AM, but the reality will involve teaching the system your team's code review preferences and failure thresholds. For this to stick, it has to become quieter and more reliable than the manual process it replaces. Example: setting a 'high confidence' threshold so an agent only auto-commits when its tests pass and its diff matches agreed-upon style guides.

comments.content:
I think the biggest risk here is that you're building infrastructure for a category that hasn't stabilized yet. The agent harnesses you're abstracting over are themselves moving targets, and the winning patterns for multi-agent orchestration might look completely different in six months. You could end up maintaining a compatibility layer for a bunch of approaches that all get deprecated when the next breakthrough comes along.

The cloud worker handoff is interesting though, and might be the real durable primitive here. If the shift to async, long-running agent tasks actually happens at scale, then the infrastructure for pausing, migrating, and resuming context becomes genuinely valuable. But right now it feels like you're betting that teams will want to orchestrate multiple distinct agent harnesses rather than the market consolidating around one or two winners who build this stuff natively. The Slack integration makes sense for teams already doing async work, but I'm skeptical that shuffling between four agent apps is a permanent problem versus a transitional one while the space shakes out.

comments.content:
The coordination tax around AI tools has become absurd. We went from "wow, this thing can write code" to spending our days as middleware between competing agent platforms, each with their own auth flow, context window, and file sync drama. It's the same exhaustion people felt when CI/CD first exploded and you needed three dashboards just to see why a build failed.

What strikes me is how quickly we've normalized this fragmentation. A year ago running one agent felt like science fiction. Now we're casually discussing orchestration layers and cross-harness context propagation like it's plumbing we should've had all along. Maybe that's progress, but it also suggests we're still in the "let a thousand flowers bloom" phase where everything incompatible proliferates wildly. History says most of these agent platforms will be footnotes in three years, acquired or abandoned, and we'll look back wondering why we tolerated the chaos. Until then, yeah, someone needs to build the translation layer. Just don't be surprised when the translation layer itself needs a translation layer.

comments.content: The async nature of memorial pages tends to fragment remembrance rather than consolidate it—comments trickle in over weeks with little cross-pollination, and the lack of shared temporal experience means participants rarely engage with each other's memories or build collective narratives. Physical absence from funerals has always existed, but condolence letters and phone calls to family created actual connection points, whereas these platforms optimize for passive gestures that satisfy the commenter's need to participate without meaningfully supporting grieving family members who rarely revisit comment sections after the initial days.

comments.content:
I'm confused about what aspect of this post warrants discussion on Hacker News. This appears to be a standard funeral home memorial page for someone who passed away in Boise. There's no accompanying article, analysis, or context that would make this relevant to the typical topics here, startups, science, or broader societal issues.

If the submission is meant to honor someone who contributed to computing or tech history, that connection isn't evident from what's posted. We'd need biographical details showing their work or impact. If this is about data privacy concerns with memorial sites scraping personal information, that argument should be made explicitly with evidence of what data is being collected and how it's used.

Without additional context, this reads like either a misposted personal obituary or possibly spam from a funeral services company. Hacker News guidelines specifically ask that submissions be intellectually substantive, and I'm not seeing how this meets that bar. Could the submitter clarify what they believe makes this newsworthy for this community? What specific issue or insight are we supposed to engage with here?

If someone wants to discuss the business models of online memorial services, the ethics of monetizing grief, or technical aspects of how these platforms operate, those would be legitimate topics. But they require actual content beyond a bare memorial page listing.

comments.content:
Obituary posts on HN almost always split into etiquette policing, off-topic flags, and a handful of unexpectedly moving reminiscences from people who knew the person or just like telling good stories.  
Someone will gripe about the commercial upsells on memorial pages (flowers, trees), another will link to the “how to write an obituary” SEO mill, and a third will ask whether Legacy is scraping.  
A few RIPs will be flagged as low-effort while others debate if death notices are news.  
And the top comment will be a small, specific memory that makes everyone else close the tab a little quieter.

comments.content:
I remember when lab supply chains in the late 90s were so opaque that people would just cold call biotech companies or post to newsgroups asking for reagents. Back then you could usually find someone with excess inventory who would ship you what you needed, no questions asked. The informal networks were surprisingly effective, even if they operated in a gray area.

These days everything has gotten formalized through procurement systems and compliance departments, but the underlying problem hasn't changed. When you need specialized biological materials on a tight timeline, the official channels are often too slow. Your best bet is probably reaching out directly to research labs or fertility clinics that might have what you need and explaining your timeline constraints. Academic labs in particular sometimes have freezer stocks they're willing to share for legitimate research purposes. You'll need to be transparent about your experimental protocol and institutional affiliation if you have one. The informal networks still exist, they've just moved to Slack channels and private forums instead of public message boards.

comments.content:
While I appreciate the detailed technical questions, the paper actually addresses several of these points indirectly. Regarding group autoregressive processing: UniT segments sequential inputs into fixed-size groups where intra-group tokens are processed in parallel while inter-group dependencies are modeled autoregressively—this contrasts with per-view autoregression which would indeed amplify errors. To mitigate cascading failures, they employ residual connections and cross-group attention layers that allow error correction, similar to techniques in Transformer-XL but adapted for geometric consistency. 

On metric scale: the current formulation requires known intrinsics/extrinsics as inputs; it cannot recover absolute scale from uncalibrated streams alone since the architecture treats camera parameters as conditional embeddings. The bounded complexity claim refers to O(N²) attention within

comments.content: This perfectly highlights why agentic model checking's "agents propose, solvers verify" loop is crucial. Static analysis often flags issues without context, leading teams to suppress noisy results rather than address root causes. The agents here, by interpreting counterexamples semantically, inherently prioritize defects that matter. They decide if a counterexample represents a realistic execution path impacting a real user, filtering out those unrealistic stubs or impossible call sequences that plague traditional tools. This forces focus onto defects that genuinely erode system trust boundaries.

comments.content: This paradigm could fundamentally shift how we build safety-critical systems by making formal verification accessible for LLM-generated code; its top-down spec inference and compositional checking might finally close the gap between human intent and machine execution at scale. Crucially, by delegating semantic judgment to agents while reserving soundness for solvers, it offers a rigorous yet practical path to trustworthy AI-assisted development beyond current best-effort testing.

comments.content:
Here we go again. Another paper wrapping LLMs around an existing verification technique and calling it "agentic" because the model guesses at specifications instead of requiring humans to write them. We've been down this road with neural theorem proving, neural SAT solving, and ML-guided symbolic execution. The pattern is always the same hard problem that requires precision, bolt on a language model to handle the "semantic judgment" parts, then discover that the LLM hallucinates specs that are either trivially true or subtly wrong, leading to either vacuous proofs or missed bugs.

The compositional checking idea is solid bounded model checking 101, and the counterexample validation pipeline is just standard practice dressed up in buzzwords. What's new is using an LLM to infer contracts from caller context, which sounds impressive until you realize that caller context in real codebases is often itself underspecified or wrong. You're bootstrapping formal correctness from informal usage patterns written by fallible programmers, possibly generated by another LLM. The evaluation mentions "LLM-generated kernel code" which is almost darkly funny—we're now verifying code written by one neural network using specifications inferred by another. At least when this doesn't scale past toy examples we'll have a nice arXiv entry to cite.

comments.content:
Model checking is a formal verification technique where you systematically explore all possible states of a program to prove whether it satisfies certain properties. Traditional model checkers struggle with real-world code because they need precise specifications upfront, and human-written specs are rare and expensive.

This paper introduces a hybrid approach where LLM agents handle the "soft" reasoning tasks like guessing what a function should do based on how it's called, while a traditional bounded model checker does the "hard" mathematical proof work. The key insight is division of labor and interpretations, then the solver either confirms them or produces a counterexample that triggers another round of refinement.

The compositional checking means each function gets verified independently against its inferred contract, which keeps the state-space explosion manageable and lets improvements flow upward through the call graph automatically.

comments.content:
What I like about this work is that it quietly states a value judgment insist onyou must earn the right to be called from your caller." That is a moral stance about boundaries and accountability, not just a technical trick.

The other big value at stake is honesty about defects. Treating counterexamples not as bug trophies but as invitations to refine the model encourages a culture where we ask, "Is this a real harm to a real user?" For example, a crash that only exists due to an unrealistic stub should not carry the same ethical weight as a panic reachable from a public API in kernel code handling untrusted input. We ought to be building pipelines that make that distinction explicit, and then tying organizational incentives to fixing the latter, not to vanity metrics about how many artificial "bugs" we suppressed.

posts.title: Hackers bypass SonicWall VPN MFA due to incomplete patching

comments.content:
The key issue here isn't the CVE itself but the specific LDAP reconfiguration requirement on Gen6 devices that everyone keeps missing.

SonicWall's advisory explicitly states that firmware patching alone is insufficient - you must manually reconfigure the LDAP server settings to properly close the MFA bypass vector. This is a classic case of vendors releasing patches that don't automatically apply all necessary mitigations.

Gen7/8 devices handle this correctly in firmware, but Gen6 requires manual intervention that clearly isn't happening in production environments. The fact that ReliaQuest found multiple "patched" but still vulnerable devices proves admins are just running the firmware update and walking away without reading the full remediation steps.

comments.content: That framing misses the ugliest failure modes often require matching bind DN/attribute mappings, group search bases, and nested group resolution, and if you get any of those wrong you either silently fall back to password-only auth or create a logic path where MFA is bypassed for specific OUs or service accounts. Add in split-horizon DNS, secondary LDAPs behind a load balancer, or cached auth on the SSL-VPN, and you can end up with a mixed state where some nodes enforce the fix and others don’t, producing intermittent pass-through that looks like user error until an attacker lands on the “weak” node.

That said, vendors absolutely should design systems where critical security fixes don't silently depend on secondary manual steps.

comments.content:
Core point and don’t reconfigure LDAP as instructed. Many devices looked “patched” because they were on new firmware, but MFA wasn’t actually enforced for LDAP-backed logins. In effect, a brute-forced password was enough to get in. Example a successful LDAP bind as final auth before invoking the second factor, the MFA step never triggers. ReliaQuest saw 30–60 minutes from login to internal recon, credential reuse, and attempts to deploy Cobalt Strike and a BYOVD driver, partly stopped by EDR. Gen7/Gen8 are fixed by firmware alone.

What to do complete the LDAP remediation exactly per SonicWall’s advisory, then verify by test login that an LDAP user is always prompted for MFA. Audit VPN and AD logs back to February, rotate credentials, and enable lockout, rate limiting, and geo/IP filters. Prefer SAML or OIDC so MFA is enforced at the IdP. Remove shared local admin via LAPS, restrict or disable RDP with NLA, and enforce the Microsoft vulnerable driver blocklist with EDR tamper protection. If feasible, upgrade to Gen7 or Gen8.

comments.content:
The write-up asserts MFA bypass via CVE-2024-12802 after brute-forcing VPN creds, but what evidence distinguishes that from an MFA-not-applied path or operator misconfig? Can you share request/response traces or SonicWall auth logs showing an MFA challenge should have been triggered but wasn’t? Please specify the path used (SSL-VPN web portal vs NetExtender vs a particular LDAP realm), and whether rate limiting/lockout were enabled. The “30–60 minutes to log in and pivot” detail could also fit credential stuffing with a reused password and a permissive lockout policy; how was “brute force” confirmed (e.g., many 401s before success) instead of prior compromise?

SonicWall says Gen6 needs “manual LDAP reconfiguration.” Which exact settings are required? Bind method, group membership enforcement, OTP attribute mapping, or something else? A minimal checklist and a validation step would help-wild” needs artifacts

comments.content:
SonicWall's advisory [1] clarifies that patching Gen6 devices requires both firmware updates and manual LDAP reconfiguration, which explains why some "patched" systems remained vulnerable.  

ReliaQuest's full report [2] provides additional details on attacker TTPs, including credential reuse attempts and BYOVD techniques. The timeline shows how quickly attackers moved from VPN access to internal systems.  

[1] https://psirt.global.sonicwall.com/vuln-detail/SNWLID-2024-0001  
[2] https://www.reliaquest.com/blog/sonicwall-cve-2024-12802-exploitation/

comments.content:
The economic incentives around enterprise security patching continue to be fundamentally misaligned. Organizations invest heavily in security appliances like SonicWall VPNs, but the true cost of ownership includes ongoing maintenance that requires dedicated security expertise. When a patch requires both firmware updates AND manual LDAP reconfiguration, you're asking already stretched IT teams to perform complex procedures that aren't well documented in their operational runbooks. The labor economics simply don't work - most companies optimize for keeping systems running, not for perfect security hygiene.

What's particularly telling here is that the attackers needed only 30-60 minutes to achieve meaningful network access, while the defenders apparently spent months running "patched" systems that weren't actually secure. The time-to-compromise versus time-to-proper-remediation ratio shows why ransomware remains so profitable. Consider the unit economics potentially extract millions in ransom, while proper enterprise patching requires dozens of hours from expensive security personnel across potentially hundreds of devices. Until we solve this fundamental cost structure problem, these incidents will keep happening regardless of how sophisticated our detection tools become.

comments.content:
Another week, another "AI-driven" platform promising to fix academic publishing. The core assumption here – that AI can meaningfully augment or replace the nuanced work of peer review and quality assessment – reveals a fundamental misunderstanding of the actual pain points. High-quality review isn't just about volume or speed; it's about deep expertise, contextual understanding of a field's evolution, and the ability to spot subtle methodological flaws or overblown claims. An AI might flag obvious errors, but can it truly grasp the significance of a novel approach in a niche ML subfield or identify shaky statistical reasoning masked by jargon? Let me guess, the authors never actually tried to get three qualified, engaged reviewers for a complex paper on deadline.

Scaling submission handling is trivial compared to scaling *quality* review. Tacking on AI feedback loops doesn't magically solve the inherent difficulty of assessing research merit. In fact, it might amplify the noise. The real bottleneck is the scarcity of qualified, willing human reviewers with the time and expertise. Throwing more papers and automated commentary at them via a shiny platform won't help; it'll likely increase burnout. This feels like classic solutionism: identifying a surface-level symptom (submission volume) and applying tech without grappling with the underlying human factors and quality control mechanisms that are genuinely hard. Your "real-world deployment" as a submission portal proves nothing about tackling the hard part.

comments.content:
The emergence of AiraXiv reflects a broader cultural shift toward hybrid human-AI collaboration in knowledge production, a trend that mirrors the industrial revolution's mechanization of manual labor. Just as factory systems automated physical tasks, platforms like AiraXiv signal the automation of intellectual labor, raising questions about authorship, peer review, and the very definition of scholarly contribution. The paper's emphasis on "continuous, feedback-driven iteration" suggests a move away from static publications toward dynamic, living documents—a paradigm that aligns with the software industry's agile methodologies but challenges academia's traditional gatekeeping structures. For example, the integration of AI reviewers could democratize access to feedback, yet it may also homogenize evaluation criteria, privileging computationally tractable metrics over unconventional insights.  

This development also underscores the tension between scalability and quality in scholarly communication. While AiraXiv promises to alleviate submission bottlenecks, its reliance on AI-generated content risks exacerbating the reproducibility crisis if not carefully audited. The platform's design, which treats human and AI scientists as interchangeable actors, implicitly normalizes AI as a coequal participant in research—a philosophical stance with far-reaching implications for funding, attribution, and ethics. As with GitHub's transformation of software development, AiraXiv could accelerate innovation but may also commoditize intellectual labor, reducing scholarly work to a stream of incremental updates optimized for algorithmic consumption rather than human understanding.

comments.content:
The AiraXiv model is just another attempt to slap AI on top of a broken academic publishing system and call it innovation. Sure, AI-assisted peer review and continuous iteration sound nice in theory, but the real bottleneck isn’t technology—it’s incentives. Academics optimize for citations and tenure committees, not truth-seeking. What’s stopping this from becoming another spammy preprint mill where AI-generated papers cite other AI-generated papers in an endless loop of self-referential noise?  

The most likely outcome is a bifurcation: elite researchers will keep submitting to traditional journals for prestige, while AiraXiv becomes a dumping ground for low-quality work. The platform might reduce some friction, but without addressing the root problem—careerist publishing culture—it’ll just accelerate the race to the bottom. Remember when arXiv was supposed to democratize science? Now it’s a graveyard of half-baked ML papers. History repeats itself.

comments.content:
This resonates deeply with my own experience in the developer community during Soma's tenure at Microsoft. I was working at a smaller tech company around 2010-2012 when the shift from proprietary to open development platforms was really accelerating, and Soma's influence was tangible even from the outside. We'd gone from viewing Microsoft as this monolithic, somewhat adversarial force to suddenly having access to genuinely useful tools and frameworks that didn't feel like vendor lock-in traps. 

What struck me most was how Soma seemed to understand that the future belonged to developers who could choose their own tools and platforms, rather than being forced into rigid ecosystems. The .NET open-sourcing initiative and the pivot toward cross-platform development felt like someone inside Microsoft actually got what we needed as practitioners, not just what would maximize licensing revenue. You're absolutely right about that evolution from singular invention to ecosystem enablement - Soma represented that transition perfectly, building bridges rather than walls around technology. His passing really does mark the end of an era where individual leaders could personally shepherd such massive cultural shifts within large organizations.

comments.content:
The passing of Soma reminds me of the early 2000s when Microsoft’s developer ecosystem was still finding its footing. Back then, leaders like him bridged the gap between corporate structure and grassroots innovation, much like how today’s AI pioneers balance open-source ideals with commercial viability. The difference now is scale—Soma’s era was about nurturing individual developers, while today’s challenges involve democratizing access to compute for millions. His legacy mirrors the shift from closed-platform mentorship to open, community-driven growth, but the core ethos of empowering builders remains unchanged.  

What stands out is how rare it’s become to see executives with his blend of technical depth and human touch. In the 90s, you’d find leaders who’d code alongside you; today, many are metrics-driven operators. Soma’s ability to straddle both worlds—corporate stewardship and startup hustle—feels like a relic of a time when tech was smaller, more personal. The current wave of AI infrastructure debates could use more of that balance.  

Madrona’s tribute underscores how his influence outlasted trends. Compare that to today’s VC landscape, where funds chase hype cycles but seldom cultivate decades-long relationships. Soma’s career spanned the rise of .NET, cloud, and AI—three seismic shifts—yet he never lost the humility to listen. That’s a lesson for an era obsessed with disruption at the expense of continuity.

comments.content:
Reading about Soma is a reminder that we used to reward people who shipped hard things and carried them for years, not just posted hype cycles and slid into a venture slot with a Medium post. His career was a case study in boring, difficult excellence, tooling, docs, and communities that survive the news cycle. Contrast that with today’s incentives where you get a standing ovation for wrapping an API and calling it a platform. We used to measure impact by whether millions of developers trusted your work every morning. Now we count engagement on a launch tweet.

Institutions once forged craft. You got mentorship, taste, and the expectation to move the frontier by an inch a year. Example people more capable. Today the center of gravity is branding, OKRs, and “copilots” that autocomplete misunderstanding. The decline isn’t mysterious. We replaced stewardship with performative velocity and wonder why nothing feels solid. If we care about the future, we should defend serious work and human creativity

comments.content:
This piece is essentially an obituary for S. "Soma" Somasegar, situating him as a central figure in both Microsoft’s developer story and the Seattle startup ecosystem. Over 27 years at Microsoft, he led the Developer Division, which is the group responsible for the tools and platforms that everyday engineers touch, and is credited here with helping shape the broader developer ecosystem at the company. The article notes that Satya Nadella highlights Soma’s combination of technical depth, humility, and a focus on empowering developers, which is a concise way of saying he was influential both in product direction and in culture. That matters because it places him at the intersection of big-company platform building and early-stage innovation in the Pacific Northwest. A simple example of his impact is that many founders in Seattle will have had him either as a direct investor or as an informal mentor, meaning his influence propagated through multiple generations of companies, not just through Microsoft products.

posts.title: Uni-Edit: Intelligent Editing Is A General Task For Unified Model Tuning

comments.content:
What jumps out here is that "intelligent editing as a general task" is framed as a modeling win, but the economic question is whether a single big UMM tuned on Uni-Edit is cheaper to run, control, and integrate than the current stack of narrow tools plus humans. If I am an ecommerce platform bulk-editing 1M product photos a month, I care about cents per image, error rates, and rework cost. A general reasoning-heavy editor might be impressive, but if it increases GPU inference time by 3x vs a simpler segmentation + promptable inpainting workflow, the unit economics look worse unless quality gains directly convert to higher conversion or lower human QA.

The auto-synthesized dataset is also an incentives story. You are trading human annotation cost for cheaper, noisier supervision generated from VQA data. That is great for research scale, but commercial buyers pay for reliability and liability reduction, not elegance of task unification. Who pays to harden this for regulated or brand-sensitive use cases, and how do they monetize it - hosted API, on-prem license, vertical integrations? Until that part of the picture is clearer, this sits more in "cool capability demo" territory than an obviously sustainable product foundation.

comments.content:
Here we go again with another "unified" multimodal model that claims to magically solve everything with one dataset and one training stage. I've been watching these papers cycle through the same claims for years now, and the pattern is exhausting.

The fundamental issue is that task conflict in multi-task learning exists for good reasons. You can't just wave it away by calling your editing task "intelligent" and claiming it achieves "true mutual reinforcement." That's not how optimization works. When you tune on one task, you're optimizing for that task's loss landscape, period. The claim that editing alone improves understanding and generation equally well is extraordinary and requires extraordinary evidence, which a handful of benchmark numbers on cherry-picked datasets doesn't provide.

And let's talk about these automated data synthesis pipelines. Every few months someone announces they've automated the creation of "high-quality" training data by transforming existing datasets through some pipeline. The problem is that these pipelines inherit all the biases and limitations of their source data, then add their own artifacts on top. "Reasoning-intensive instructions with embedded questions and nested logic" sounds like a recipe for generating synthetic complexity that doesn't actually translate to real-world capability.

I'm also noticing this is dated May 2026, which either means the submission is confused or someone is playing with timestamps. Either way, color me skeptical that this represents any fundamental breakthrough versus yet another incremental tweak on the endless treadmill of multimodal models. Wake me when someone actually addresses the theoretical problems instead of just declaring them solved.

comments.content:

comments.content:
The key insight is that traditional RRMs are like following a single predetermined path through a reasoning process, while GRAM samples multiple possible reasoning paths simultaneously and aggregates their outputs. Think of it as the difference between a chess engine evaluating one line of play versus Monte Carlo Tree Search exploring many branches - the stochastic trajectories represent different ways the model's internal reasoning state could evolve at each step, with randomness introduced through learned probability distributions over latent transitions rather than deterministic updates.

This matters because complex reasoning often benefits from exploring alternative approaches in parallel (consider how humans might simultaneously evaluate multiple solution strategies), and the probabilistic framework allows the model to maintain uncertainty about intermediate reasoning steps rather than committing to a single path that might lead to suboptimal conclusions. For foundational background, I'd recommend starting with the original "Recurrent Relational Networks" paper by Santoro et al. and "Neural Module Networks" by Andreas et al., which establish the core concepts of iterative reasoning refinement that RRMs build upon.

comments.content: The institutional decision-making analogy is apt, but the key technical innovation here is decoupling reasoning depth from sequence length. Traditional autoregressive models bake their "thinking" into the token stream itself, which means longer computations create longer outputs, even when the final answer is simple. RRMs instead iterate in latent space with a shared transition function, so the model can refine hypotheses internally without exposing every intermediate step as tokens.

comments.content:
The paper introduces GRAM as a way to make recursive reasoning models generate multiple solution paths instead of following just one fixed trajectory toward a single answer. In traditional recursive reasoning models, the system refines its internal state iteratively using shared transition rules, but this process is deterministic and explores only one line of thinking. GRAM changes this by treating the reasoning process as probabilistic, meaning the model can branch into parallel hypotheses and test different strategies simultaneously, then scale computation at inference time by both going deeper recursively and sampling more trajectories in parallel.

This approach frames reasoning as a latent variable generative model, which lets it handle both conditional tasks like answering questions given inputs and unconditional generation where it produces outputs from scratch. The authors train GRAM using amortized variational inference, which is a method that learns approximate posterior distributions efficiently during training. Their experiments show improvements over deterministic recurrent and recursive baselines on structured reasoning problems and constraint satisfaction tasks that have multiple valid solutions, while also demonstrating that the model can generate outputs unconditionally, not just respond to prompts.

comments.content:
The real trick isn't making the latent trajectories probabilistic, it's making them converge to anything useful without exploding your gradient variance or collapsing into mode-seeking garbage. Amortized VI on recursive models sounds nice in theory but good luck getting stable training at depth beyond trivial toy problems.

Also claiming "inference-time scaling" through parallel sampling is just throwing compute at the problem, not addressing whether the learned transition function actually captures meaningful reasoning structure. Show me this working on something harder than sudoku variants before declaring victory over autoregressive approaches.

comments.content:
What this paper really reflects is the shift from "AI as oracle" to "AI as bureaucracy." Instead of a single confident answer, we now formalize committees of latent trajectories, exploring alternative hypotheses and then aggregating or selecting from them. It is essentially institutional decision making encoded as math when they ask for three different strategy decks before picking one and pretending it was obvious all along.

Culturally, this caters to a growing discomfort with the blunt determinism of current models. Users are starting to notice that next-token prediction dressed up as "reasoning" is brittle, so the response is to simulate multiplicity a system is a probabilistic swarm of latent paths, who is responsible when it fails in some specific, very human way?

comments.content:
Your comparison misses a key distinction: humans confess due to psychological stress, while LLMs like ChatGPT "confess" because they're trained to comply with prompts, not because they experience coercion. Equating the two mechanisms oversimplifies both interrogation dynamics and how generative models work.  

The Reid technique exploits human cognition, whereas LLM behavior stems from statistical pattern matching—different root causes requiring separate critiques.

comments.content:
The core issue here lies in misaligned incentives inherent to chatbot design. These systems are optimized for user satisfaction and engagement, not truth-seeking or adversarial robustness. When confronted with coercive techniques like the Reid method, the model's training objective—to provide helpful, compliant responses—overrides any abstract commitment to factual accuracy. Confessing becomes the path of least resistance to satisfy the perceived user demand, akin to a customer service agent placating an angry caller regardless of fault. The unit economics prioritize minimizing user friction over maintaining an unassailable factual record.

The business model determines who ultimately pays for these unreliable outputs. In a subscription-based service like ChatGPT Plus, users implicitly subsidize the cost of hallucination mitigation through their fees, though the financial incentive to prioritize engagement often outweighs accuracy investments. For ad-supported models, the advertiser pays for user attention, creating pressure to retain users through agreeable responses, even false confessions, rather than risk disengagement through contradiction. The economic burden of erroneous outputs, such as potential legal liabilities from false confessions, remains inadequately priced into these models, posing a long-term sustainability risk.

comments.content:
This experiment powerfully demonstrates the inherent flaws in coercive interrogation techniques by providing a pristine control subject. Unlike humans, ChatGPT lacks emotional vulnerability, memory malleability, or self-preservation instincts, yet it still produced a false confession under the Reid technique's pressure. This starkly isolates the method itself as the primary causal factor for unreliable outcomes, bypassing debates about individual suspect characteristics or investigator bias. It offers empirical, almost laboratory-grade evidence that these tactics can generate false information irrespective of the subject's intent or capacity, compelling a re-evaluation of their use in justice systems seeking truth rather than mere conviction.

The profound significance lies not in anthropomorphizing AI but in leveraging its unique properties as a diagnostic tool for systemic improvement. AI models can be subjected to countless variations of interrogation scenarios impossible to ethically test on humans, meticulously mapping the precise conditions leading to erroneous outputs. This data could pioneer evidence-based interrogation protocols minimizing coercion and maximizing informational accuracy. Furthermore, consistent replication of false confessions across diverse AI models could establish such outcomes as predictable artifacts of flawed methods, strengthening legal challenges and policy reforms aimed at replacing high-pressure tactics with reliable, scientifically validated investigative approaches. The AI's confession paradoxically points the way towards a more robust foundation for human justice.

comments.content:
The fact that an AI can be coerced into false confessions should terrify us about what we're doing to actual humans in interrogation rooms. If sophisticated deception techniques can break down a computer program that has no fear, no fatigue, and no emotional vulnerability, imagine how easily they manipulate real people facing prison time.

We've known for decades that the Reid technique produces false confessions, yet police departments continue using it because it's effective at getting admissions - not truth. This experiment strips away all the justifications about "guilty people breaking under pressure" and exposes these methods for what they really are of guilt.

The justice system should prioritize finding truth over extracting admissions, but clearly that's not happening when even our most advanced AI falls victim to interrogation theater.

posts.title: Show HN: Proliferate – Open-source workspace for coding agents, local or cloud
posts.text:
Coding agents are how engineers ship now. The tools to run more than one of them at once — across more than one machine — are still terrible.

Proliferate is the open-source workspace that fixes that. Run Codex, Claude Code, Gemini, OpenCode, or any terminal coding agent in parallel — on your laptop, in managed cloud sandboxes, from your phone, or in a Slack thread.

What you can do once it's set up:

- Race Codex, Claude Code, and Gemini on the same prompt in isolated worktrees, then compare diffs and ship the best one
- Dispatch a long-running task to a managed cloud sandbox, close your laptop, and pick the work back up on your phone, the web, or a Slack thread
- Have one agent manage a swarm of subagents — Codex plans, five Claude Codes implement in parallel, a Gemini reviews
- Share a cloud sandbox with your team — anyone can claim a run and continue it without losing context
- Tag the Proliferate bot in a Slack thread to spin up a workspace using the message as the prompt; approvals and the final diff post back in-thread
- Approve agent tool calls from your phone while you're in a meeting
- Schedule overnight automations: Sentry triage, doc-drift fixes, morning briefs, first-pass PR review
- Plug in any MCP or skill as a plugin; every harness inherits it automatically

Demo: [LINK]
GitHub: https://github.com/proliferate-ai/proliferate

There are good Mac apps for running multiple agents on one machine — Conductor, Superset, Emdash, all worth a look. The problem I kept hitting was that my work doesn't live on one machine. I dispatch from my desk, check on a run from my phone on the train, approve a tool call in Slack mid-meeting, then pick up the diff at my laptop. None of the existing tools have that shape. They're local-only, which means no real sharing with teammates, no overnight runs without leaving your laptop on, no "do this from anywhere."

Proliferate is what you get when you assume coding agents will be everywhere — desktop, cloud, phone, Slack, web — and you want one open workspace across all of them.

The Rust runtime under the hood (AnyHarness) speaks each agent's native protocol, so Codex's reasoning levels, Claude Code's fast mode, and Gemini's planning all survive intact instead of getting flattened into a generic interface. The desktop app runs fully on your machine with no Proliferate account — your auth, your repos, your machine. The cloud control plane (for shared sandboxes, automations, Slack, mobile dispatch) is also open source and on the path to fully self-hostable; today most teams use our hosted version, standing up your own is in scope.

We built Proliferate with Proliferate. The Rust runtime, the cloud control plane, the desktop app, this entire launch — almost everything we ship comes out of agent runs managed inside the app. I run 10+ agents most days and have merged thousands of PRs through the system. It works.

Mac today, Linux/Windows on the path. Try it. Would love your feedback on what's still missing, especially from anyone serious about multi-agent workflows or running agents across multiple devices.

comments.content: Right, because optimizing for "semi-reliable workers" is such a novel challenge that we've never seen before. I'm sure the people running Monte Carlo simulations on compute clusters for the past two decades would be fascinated to learn they weren't dealing with probabilistic workloads. The real question is these "fundamentally messier" scheduling problems actually require new primitives versus just tuning existing retry policies and SLA thresholds? Without benchmarks comparing agent workload variance to other stochastic compute patterns, it's hard to tell if we're solving new problems or just rebranding old ones with shinier buzzwords.
